origin: Garden hybrid (Saponaria ocymoides × S. cypria); parent rock soapwort from the mountains of southern Europe
habitat: Rocky, sunny limestone slopes and screes of southern Europe (parent species)
blurb: A rock-garden and wall spiller that vanishes under a sheet of vivid rose-pink flowers in summer. 'Max Frei' is a tidy, long-blooming soapwort hybrid for hot, sharp-drained, xeric spots where it tumbles over stone.
water: Low; xeric once established — needs sharp drainage
spread: A trailing, mounding mat that spills over walls and rocks; clump-forming and easy to keep in bounds
seasons: Semi-evergreen green mat smothered in bright rose-pink flowers through summer; tidy foliage the rest of the season
wildlife: Bees and butterflies work the summer flowers en masse
toxic: Contains saponins — mildly toxic if eaten in quantity; a historic soap plant, not a food
gaps: Photos are species-representative of the parent rock soapwort (Saponaria ocymoides) — no open-licensed photographs of the hybrid 'Max Frei' / S. × lembergii exist; the cultivar looks essentially identical (a brighter, tidier rose-pink form).

care.hardiness: Hardy well into the cold — the parent rock soapwort is rated USDA zones 2–9 — so 'Max Frei' is bulletproof across Boulder and the Front Range (zones 5b–6a). It thrives in our high-altitude sun and dry summers.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant nursery transplants in spring after the mid-May last frost on a sharply drained, sunny site — the top of a wall, a rock garden, a gravel bed or the front edge of a border where it can trail. In containers use gritty, free-draining mix; it overwinters well in a pot given sharp drainage.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun. It wants all-day sun for the densest mat and heaviest bloom, and gets leggy and shy-flowering in shade.[1][2]
care.soil: Average to dry, well-drained soil — sandy, gravelly or lean loam — and it actively prefers neutral to slightly alkaline ground, so Boulder's limy soils suit it. The one thing it will not tolerate is heavy, wet clay.[1][2]
care.water: Low. Genuinely xeric once established, thriving on natural precipitation with only occasional deep soaks in extended summer drought; overwatering in poorly drained soil is the surest way to lose it.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space transplants 12–15 in apart; the trailing stems knit and spill quickly to cover a wall face or gravel slope.[1][2]
care.propagation: Increase by division of the clump in spring or summer, or by softwood cuttings; the species self-sows freely from seed, but the hybrid is grown vegetatively to keep it true.[1][2]
care.bloom: Sprays of small, five-petaled, vivid rose-pink flowers (about 1/2–3/4 in wide) blanket the mat through summer — roughly late spring into August here — often nearly hiding the foliage at peak.[1][2]
care.feeding: None needed. Lean soil keeps it compact and free-flowering; rich soil and fertilizer make it floppy.[1]
care.maintenance: Shear the whole mat back by about half right after the main flush of bloom — this keeps it tidy and compact and often coaxes a lighter rebloom. Little else is needed.[1][2]
care.troubles: Essentially pest- and disease-free and deer-resistant. The main failure mode is crown and root rot in wet, heavy soil or from overwatering; give it full sun and sharp drainage and it is trouble-free.[1][2]
care.companions: A classic wall and rock-garden spiller with other xeric sun-lovers — dianthus, sedums, creeping thyme, aubrieta and dwarf bulbs — tumbling over stone and gravel.[1]

edible.summary: Soapwort is a soap plant, not a food — it contains saponins and should not be eaten.[1][2]
edible.caution: Saponaria means 'soap': the leaves and roots are rich in saponins that lather when bruised or boiled in water, the historic use of the plant. Those same saponins are mildly toxic if eaten in any quantity (they irritate the gut and can break down red blood cells), so no part of the plant is a food. Grow 'Max Frei' as an ornamental groundcover only, and keep it off the plate.[1][2]
